title: Oral Exam: Societal Debate (B1/B2)
question: Norwegian cities are growing rapidly. Should we build more high-rise buildings in city centers to save natural areas outside the cities?
explanation: This is a classic dilemma in urban planning. By building upwards, we can provide housing for many more people in a small area, which limits the need to build over forests and fields around cities. On the other hand, many believe that high-rises destroy the city's historical character and create cold, windy street environments.

title: Oral Exam: Working Life (B1/B2)
question: Many experience that the line between work and private life is being blurred due to smartphones. How does this affect our mental health?
explanation: When we are always available on email and Teams, the brain never completely rests. This can lead to a feeling of constant stress and, in the worst case, burnout. To preserve good mental health, it is crucial that both employees and managers respect free time and set clear boundaries for when to log off.

title: Oral Exam: Environment (B1/B2)
question: Should it be mandatory for clothing chains to accept and repair old clothes they have sold?
explanation: Such a mandate would force the clothing industry to take responsibility for the product's entire life cycle, not just the profit from the sale. It would encourage better quality clothing and reduce the enormous amount of textile waste. The challenge is whether small shops will have the resources to implement such repair services in practice.

title: Oral Exam: Education (B1/B2)
question: Is it right that parents' finances largely affect children's school performance in Norway?
explanation: Even though school is free, we see a clear social inheritance. Parents with higher education and good finances often have more capacity to follow up on homework and pay for extracurricular activities that stimulate learning. To create true equality, the school must be better able to support children who do not get this support from home.

title: Oral Exam: Health (B1/B2)
question: The GP scheme is in crisis. Should municipalities be allowed to hire doctors as permanent salaried employees instead of doctors running their own businesses?
explanation: Many young doctors want the security of a fixed salary and normal working hours rather than being self-employed with significant administrative responsibility. By offering permanent positions, municipalities can make the profession more attractive. The counterargument is that it may become more expensive for the state and less flexible for patients.

title: Oral Exam: Technology (B1/B2)
question: Norway is far ahead in digitalization. Does this create challenges for our privacy?
explanation: Yes, the more we digitalize, the more data is collected about us by both authorities and private companies. There is a risk that information can be misused or lost in data breaches. We must have extremely strict legislation and good oversight bodies like Datatilsynet to ensure that efficiency does not come at the expense of the individual's right to privacy.

title: Oral Exam: Societal Debate (B1/B2)
question: In Norway, we have high trust in the authorities. What happens to society if this trust disappears?
explanation: Trust is the glue in Norwegian society. If trust disappears, it becomes harder to implement necessary political reforms, people become less willing to pay taxes, and we see more polarization and conflict. To preserve trust, transparency, low corruption, and politicians actually keeping their promises are crucial.

title: Oral Exam: Working Life (B1/B2)
question: What significance does an inclusive working environment have for the productivity of a company?
explanation: An inclusive environment where everyone feels seen and valued, regardless of background, leads to lower sick leave and higher motivation. When employees feel safe, they dare to share ideas and collaborate better. Diversity also brings in different perspectives that can make the company more innovative and better equipped to solve complex tasks.

title: Oral Exam: Environment (B1/B2)
question: Should we stop building new roads and instead use the money to maintain the ones we already have?
explanation: Maintaining existing roads is often more environmentally friendly than blasting out new nature for highways. There is also a major challenge with massive decay on many Norwegian county roads that threatens traffic safety. At the same time, some new road projects are necessary to connect regions and ensure efficient freight transport.

title: Oral Exam: Education (B1/B2)
question: In many countries, students must pay for all materials themselves. Should learning materials in higher education be free in Norway?
explanation: Even though studies are free, books and digital equipment cost thousands of kroner each semester. For students from low-income families, this can be a major barrier. Free digital learning materials would strengthen the principle of equal right to education. The counterargument is that it would cost the state a lot, and students might take less care of things that are free.

title: Oral Exam: Societal Debate (B1/B2)
question: Norwegian men take more responsibility at home than before. Have we now achieved full equality in the family?
explanation: We have come a long way, but statistics show that women still do most of the 'invisible' work, such as planning children's clothes, birthdays, and logistics. But compared to many other countries, the Norwegian father role is unique, with fathers active in diaper changes and parent meetings. True equality requires a continued change in both attitudes and expectations.

title: Oral Exam: Health (B1/B2)
question: Is it right to ban advertising for unhealthy food and drink aimed at adults, not just children?
explanation: Lifestyle diseases linked to poor diet cost the health service billions. A ban on advertising for, for example, extremely sugary drinks could help people make better choices. On the other hand, many would argue that adults must have personal responsibility and the freedom to decide their own diet without state paternalism.

title: Oral Exam: Working Life (B1/B2)
question: How can we prevent young people from falling out of the workforce and ending up on disability benefits?
explanation: We must have closer cooperation between schools, the health service, and NAV. Early help for mental challenges is key. In addition, the working world must become more flexible and open to people who do not have 100 percent work capacity. Everyone needs to feel that they contribute and are part of a community to have a good quality of life.

title: Oral Exam: Environment (B1/B2)
question: Norway exports a lot of salmon. What are the environmental challenges with the aquaculture industry?
explanation: The biggest challenges are sea lice, which threaten wild salmon, and emissions of feed residues and feces that pollute the seabed under the cages. In addition, the escape of farmed fish is a problem for genetic diversity. The industry must invest heavily in closed systems and more sustainable feed to be able to grow in the future.

title: Oral Exam: Technology (B1/B2)
question: Should algorithms be allowed to decide whether a person is granted a loan or insurance?
explanation: Algorithms can be more objective and faster than humans, but they can also carry hidden biases based on historical data. It is crucial that such processes are transparent and that a person always has the right to a human assessment and an explanation of why they were rejected.

title: Oral Exam: Societal Debate (B1/B2)
question: Is Norwegian society too focused on academic achievements and too little on practical knowledge?
explanation: It may seem like we have developed a 'master's disease' where everyone thinks they need a long university education to gain respect. This leads to a shortage of skilled workers and practical talent not being valued enough. We must elevate professional pride and show that practical knowledge is just as complex and important as theoretical knowledge.

title: Oral Exam: Leisure (B1/B2)
question: Why is it important that children get to participate in unorganized play without adult interference?
explanation: In unorganized play, children learn to negotiate, solve conflicts, and set their own boundaries. They develop creativity and independence when they have to invent things themselves. If adults always direct the play, children miss out on important social learning processes that are necessary to become confident and functioning adults.

title: Oral Exam: Health (B1/B2)
question: Many elderly people dread the digitalization of health services. How can we ensure that everyone gets the help they need?
explanation: We must have a combination of good digital tools and human contact. Those who do not master the technology must still have the opportunity to call or show up physically. Training eldery in digital competence is important, but we must never forget that care is about human relations that cannot be replaced by an app.

title: Oral Exam: Working Life (B1/B2)
question: What are the dangers of an excessively high use of overtime in the workplace?
explanation: Too much overtime over a long period leads to exhausted employees, poorer quality of work, and an increased risk of errors and accidents. it also hits family life and social health hard. The Working Environment Act therefore has strict rules for overtime to protect employees from exploitation and ensure a sustainable working life.

title: Oral Exam: Environment (B1/B2)
question: Should it become more expensive to buy imported goods that we also produce in Norway, such as apples or meat?
explanation: By placing tariffs on imported goods, we protect the Norwegian farmer and ensure our own food security. It is also more environmentally friendly to eat locally sourced food. On the other hand, it will lead to higher food prices for consumers, which particularly affects those with low incomes in a time of general price increases.

title: Oral Exam: Technology (B1/B2)
question: How do social media affect our ability to conduct a factual and respectful debate?
explanation: Anonymity and lack of face-to-face contact make the threshold for writing nasty things much lower on the internet. Algorithms also amplify extreme opinions because they create more engagement. This often leads to nuances disappearing, and the debate becomes polarized and characterized by personal attacks instead of professional arguments.

title: Oral Exam: Societal Debate (B1/B2)
question: Norway has one of the world's highest proportions of the population in work. Why is this so important for our economy?
explanation: High employment means that many pay taxes and few receive benefits. This is the very foundation for being able to finance our generous welfare state with free schools and healthcare. Every person who moves from benefits to a job is an enormous economic gain for society and a major personal victory for the individual.

title: Oral Exam: Culture (B1/B2)
question: Why is the concept of 'friluftsliv' (outdoor life) so central to the Norwegian soul?
explanation: Outdoor life is about more than just exercise; it is a philosophy about finding peace and meaning in nature. It is deeply rooted in our history as a people who have lived off harvesting from the sea and the forest. Today, it serves as an important counterweight to modern, technological life and gives us a sense of national identity and shared cultural heritage.

title: Oral Exam: Working Life (B1/B2)
question: Should employers have the right to test employees for drugs without suspicion?
explanation: In professions where safety is critical, such as pilots or bus drivers, zero tolerance and testing are completely necessary to protect lives. But in standard office jobs, many would argue that this is an unacceptable intrusion into private life. Trust between employee and manager is a pillar of Norwegian working life that can be damaged by excessive control.

title: Oral Exam: Environment (B1/B2)
question: Is it right that Norway subsidizes electric car buyers when most who can afford an electric car are already wealthy?
explanation: This is a classic question of fairness. Subsidizing has been extremely effective in replacing the car fleet and reaching climate goals. But it creates a sense of injustice when the rich get cheaper luxury cars, while the poor have to pay high taxes on their older petrol cars. Perhaps the support should now be more targeted at those with lower incomes.

title: Oral Exam: Health (B1/B2)
question: Why is the incidence of allergies and food intolerances increasing in the Western world?
explanation: Researchers point to several possible causes, such as environments that are too sterile in childhood, changed diets with more ultra-processed food, and pollution. It may also be that we are more aware and better at diagnosing such ailments now than before. Regardless, it leads to major challenges for both individuals and for large kitchens in schools and kindergartens.

title: Oral Exam: Education (B1/B2)
question: Should the grade for conduct and order be removed from Norwegian schools?
explanation: Supporters believe that the grade is outdated and subjective, and that it punishes students who may be struggling with challenges at home or have diagnoses. Opponents believe the grade is an important signal to students about the importance of good manners and punctuality, which are crucial skills in the working world.

title: Oral Exam: Societal Debate (B1/B2)
question: How can we best integrate refugees who come to Norway with little schooling background?
explanation: We must have a more practical approach to Norwegian language training, preferably out in workplaces from day one. The combination of language training and meaningful work is the fastest path to integration. In addition, we must recognize the real-world competence they bring with them, even if they don't have formal diplomas, so they can use their resources.

title: Oral Exam: Technology (B1/B2)
question: Will robots in the future be able to replace human care in elderly care?
explanation: Robots can take over many heavy, physical lifts and practical tasks like medicine distribution and cleaning. This can free up time for caregivers to actually talk to the elderly. But true human closeness, empathy, and understanding can never be replaced by a machine. Care is deeply about relationships between people.

title: Oral Exam: Environment (B1/B2)
question: Should Norway stop exporting weapons to countries involved in war?
explanation: This is a heavy ethical question. On the one hand, we want to be a peace nation and avoid Norwegian weapons being used for human rights violations. On the other hand, the defense industry is important for Norwegian technology development and jobs. Norway already has one of the world's strictest regulatory frameworks, but the debate constantly continues on whether we follow it strictly enough.

title: Oral Exam: Societal Debate (B1/B2)
question: Are social differences in Norway becoming too large, and what are the consequences of this?
explanation: Even though Norway is more equal than most countries, we see the richest pulling away while many struggle with increased living costs. Large differences weaken trust in society and can lead to more unrest and crime. It is important to maintain strong redistribution policy through the tax system and good public services for all.

title: Oral Exam: Working Life (B1/B2)
question: Why is it important to have a statutory minimum wage in certain industries in Norway?
explanation: Norway has no general minimum wage, but in industries with a lot of foreign labor, such as construction and cleaning, the wage is universally applied. This is absolutely crucial to prevent social dumping and ensure that everyone working in Norway has a wage they can live on. It also protects Norwegian companies from unfair competition from unscrupulous actors.

title: Oral Exam: Environment (B1/B2)
question: Should we introduce a national maximum limit for how much electricity a household can use at a subsidized price?
explanation: Such a measure would give people a strong incentive to save electricity and invest in energy measures like heat pumps and insulation. It is fair that one gets cheap electricity for basic needs, while luxury consumption, such as heating swimming pools or large cabins, should cost more. The challenge is setting the limit so that families with children and those in old, drafty houses are not punished unreasonably hard.

title: Oral Exam: Education (B1/B2)
question: What are the benefits of taking a year at a Folk High School before starting higher education?
explanation: Folk High School gives students a chance to mature humanly and socially without grade pressure. One can immerse oneself in an interest, build lifelong friendships, and learn to live with others. This often makes students better equipped for the demanding studies that come later because they are more confident in themselves and their choices.

title: Oral Exam: Societal Debate (B1/B2)
question: In Norway, many live alone. Is this a positive development showing freedom, or a sign of increasing loneliness?
explanation: It is both. For many, it means freedom to shape their own life and be independent. At the same time, we are social creatures, and living alone increases the risk of social isolation, especially among the elderly and young men. We must build cities and housing that facilitate community and meeting places, even if one has their own front door.

title: Oral Exam: Health (B1/B2)
question: Should it be mandatory to label retouched images of bodies and faces on social media?
explanation: Yes, this is an important measure against the enormous body pressure that especially young girls experience. By labeling the images, it becomes clear that the 'perfection' is fake and created by a filter. This can contribute to better mental health and a more realistic relationship with one's own appearance. Legislation in Norway has already taken steps in this direction.

title: Oral Exam: Working Life (B1/B2)
question: Why is it so difficult for many highly educated immigrants to get relevant jobs in Norway?
explanation: Many employers are unsure about foreign diplomas and require 'Norwegian work experience', which is impossible to get without the first job. In addition, language requirements and unconscious biases play a major role. Society loses enormous resources when doctors and engineers drive taxis or clean instead of using their actual expertise.

title: Oral Exam: Environment (B1/B2)
question: Should plastic packaging on fruits and vegetables be banned in Norwegian shops?
explanation: Plastic is a major environmental challenge, and much of the packaging is unnecessary. But plastic also has a function; it increases shelf life and reduces food waste significantly. We must therefore find a balance or develop more sustainable materials that protect the food as well as plastic without harming nature for hundreds of years.

title: Oral Exam: Technology (B1/B2)
question: How can artificial intelligence help us in the fight against climate change?
explanation: AI can analyze enormous amounts of data to optimize energy use in buildings, predict extreme weather, and make industrial processes much more efficient. It can also help us design new, more environmentally friendly materials. Technology is not the whole solution, but it is an extremely powerful tool that can accelerate the green transition.

title: Oral Exam: Societal Debate (B1/B2)
question: Why is it important to preserve the small local shops in rural districts, even if they are not always profitable?
explanation: The local shop is often the only social meeting place in small villages. If the shop disappears, the glue in the local community also disappears, and it becomes less attractive to live there. The state supports many of these shops financially (through the Merkur program) because they are critical for maintaining settlement throughout Norway.

title: Oral Exam: Culture (B1/B2)
question: Have streaming services like Netflix and Spotify made us more culturally poor because we only see what is popular right now?
explanation: On the one hand, we have gained access to almost all the world's culture for a low price. On the other hand, we are governed by algorithms that mean we often end up seeing the same thing as everyone else. We may lose the narrow, local, and unique cultural expressions if we do not actively look for them outside 'top 10' lists.

title: Oral Exam: Working Life (B1/B2)
question: What role should trade unions play in a modern working life characterized by individual freedom?
explanation: Even though many want individual freedom, collective rights are the very foundation of our security. Unions are needed to ensure fair wage negotiations and protect against abuse of power from large companies. The Norwegian model works precisely because we have strong organizations that balance the interests of both owners and employees.

title: Oral Exam: Health (B1/B2)
question: Should the state cover the costs of glasses for all children, regardless of family income?
explanation: Good vision is a prerequisite for learning and mastery at school. Requiring parents to pay thousands of kroner for glasses creates social differences and can lead to children going without glasses because the family cannot afford them. This is a small investment for society that has enormous significance for the individual child's future.

title: Oral Exam: Environment (B1/B2)
question: Why is it so difficult to get people to use public transport if they own a car?
explanation: The car represents enormous freedom and convenience that is difficult to compete with. Public transport must be cheaper, faster, and more reliable than the car for people to switch. In addition, many are dependent on cars in a hectic everyday life with daycare pick-ups and shopping, especially where public transport is lacking.

title: Oral Exam: Education (B1/B2)
question: Should it be mandatory with one year abroad for all students in higher education?
explanation: An exchange year gives invaluable cultural insight, language training, and an international network. It makes students more attractive in the labor market and opens up their worldview. At the same time, many have obligations at home, such as children or work, that make it impossible to travel away. Exchange should therefore be strongly encouraged, but not forced.

title: Oral Exam: Societal Debate (B1/B2)
question: Norway has a lot of hydropower. Is it right that we export this electricity to Europe when it leads to higher prices for Norwegian consumers?
explanation: This is a burning political topic. By linking us to the European market, we ensure power supply in dry years and earn money from exports. But the price is that we lose the historical advantage of cheap power for Norwegian industry and households. Many believe we must prioritize our own inhabitants higher in times of crisis.

title: Oral Exam: Health (B1/B2)
question: Why is loneliness in society increasing at the same time as we are more interconnected than ever through the internet?
explanation: Digital contact can never replace physical presence and deep, human connection. On social media, we often see only the highlight reel of others' lives, which can reinforce the feeling of being a failure and alone. True community requires time, vulnerability, and physical presence that the screen cannot give us.

title: Oral Exam: Working Life (B1/B2)
question: Should we introduce a shorter retirement age for those in physically heavy professions?
explanation: This is often called the 'sliter-ordning' (struggler scheme). Someone who has worked on roofs or in healthcare for 40 years has a body that is often exhausted before reaching 67. It is fair that those who have built the country with their hands get a dignified retirement time while they still have health to enjoy it, rather than being forced onto disability benefits at the end of their career.

title: Oral Exam: Environment (B1/B2)
question: Should it become illegal to burn oil and gas for heating homes in Norway?
explanation: In Norway, we already have a ban on the use of fossil fuel oil for heating buildings, which has been a very effective climate measure. We must continue to facilitate renewable alternatives such as district heating and electricity. The challenge is that the power grid in some areas is under heavy pressure, and we need stable energy in winter.

title: Oral Exam: Societal Debate (B1/B2)
question: Why is it important that we have a strong defense in Norway, despite the fact that we are a small country?
explanation: A strong defense acts as a deterrent and ensures our sovereignty and territorial integrity. Even though we are small, we are strategically placed in the north. Through membership in NATO, we have security guarantees, but we must also have the capacity ourselves to monitor our areas and contribute to common security in Europe.
